topic: advice about a young customer going through a breakup

turns.content: I need advice about something delicate... it is about one of my customers. a young man
Of course. What's going on with him?

his name is Emre... he is maybe 24, 25. he has been coming to me for 3 years now, every two weeks. a good kid, polite, always asks about my day. today he sat in the chair and I could see immediately... something was wrong. the eyes, you know. a barber sees everything in the mirror

You're right — the barber's chair is a uniquely honest place. People can't hide what they're feeling when someone is that close, working on them, looking at them in the mirror. So what happened with Emre?

I asked him... 'Emre, what is wrong, you look like you lost your best friend.' and he said... 'worse, Mehmet abi. Elif left me.' his girlfriend of 4 years. she ended it last week. he was trying not to cry in my chair... a grown man, 24 years old, trying to hold it together while I am cutting his hair

That's heartbreaking. Four years is a significant relationship at that age — that's basically his entire adult romantic life so far. And the fact that he's trying not to cry in your chair tells you how much pain he's in but also how much he trusts you. He didn't cancel the appointment. He came.

What did you say to him?

that is the thing... I did not know what to say. I said something about time healing, you know, the usual things. but it felt... empty. like giving someone a bandage when they have a broken bone. I want to say the right thing next time he comes. I am not his father but... in some ways, you know, a barber is like a father to his young customers. they tell us things they do not tell anyone

That's a beautiful way to see your role — and honestly, for a lot of young men, the barber IS the person they open up to. Many guys don't have a therapist, they don't talk to their friends about feelings, but they'll talk to their barber. You're already doing the most important thing: being present and caring enough to ask.

Here's what I'd suggest for next time:

1. **Don't try to fix it** — you can't, and he knows that. Just being heard matters more than any advice.
2. **Validate the pain** — something like "4 years is a long time. It is okay to feel broken right now. This is normal."
3. **Don't rush to the silver lining** — avoid "you'll find someone better" or "you're young, plenty of time." Even if true, it minimizes what he's feeling right now.
4. **Share a little of yourself** if it feels right — not advice, but a moment of connection. Something real.

The fact that he came to his appointment tells you he wanted normalcy AND he wanted someone safe. You're already that person.

yes... you are right about the silver lining thing. that is what I did wrong. I said 'you are young, there will be others.' he smiled but it was not a real smile. I could see it was the wrong thing. but you know... in my generation we did not talk about feelings like this. when my heart was broken at 22, my father said 'drink your tea and go to work.' that was the therapy, hah

Ha — "drink your tea and go to work" is honestly the summary of an entire generation's approach to emotional pain. And it works in the sense that routine keeps you upright — but it doesn't actually help you process anything.

The fact that you recognize that your generation's approach falls short and you want to do better for Emre — that says a lot about you. You're not repeating the pattern.

Next time he comes in, you could start simple: "Emre, how are you really doing?" The word "really" tells him you actually want to know, not just the polite version. And then just listen. Let him talk while you cut. The rhythm of the haircut gives him permission to open up without it feeling like a therapy session.

how are you really doing... yes, that is good. the 'really' makes the difference. and you are right about the rhythm... when I am cutting, there is a flow. the scissors, the comb, the quiet... it creates a safe space. I have had men tell me things in that chair they would never say to their wife or their doctor. the mirror helps too... they do not have to look at you directly, they talk to your reflection. it is easier that way

That insight about the mirror is remarkable — you're absolutely right. Talking to a reflection creates just enough distance to say difficult things. Therapists would call that "reduced face-threat" or something clinical, but you figured it out from 40 years in the chair. The angle of contact matters. Side by side, looking at reflections, with the ritual of the cut happening — it's safer than sitting across from someone staring at you.

You already have the perfect setup for this. You don't need to change anything about how you work — just adjust the words.

you know... we have a saying. 'dost acı söyler'... a true friend speaks bitter truths. but I think sometimes a true friend also stays quiet and just holds the space. maybe that is what Emre needs. not a proverb, not wisdom... just someone who does not look away from his pain

That might be the wisest thing you could say — and it contradicts the idea that you don't know what to tell him. You clearly do. Not looking away from someone's pain is one of the hardest and most generous things a person can do. Most people rush to fill the silence with advice because the silence is uncomfortable for THEM, not for the person hurting.

Emre is lucky to have you in that chair.

I hope so... he comes again next Friday. I will ask him 'how are you really doing' and then I will shut my mouth and listen. and cut his hair properly, because even a broken heart deserves a good haircut, hah. okay my friend. teşekkürler. this was helpful. you know... 40 years cutting hair and I am still learning how to talk to people. life is funny like that

That's the thing — the learning never stops, not after 40 years, not after 50. The fact that you're still thinking about how to be better at the human side of your work is why customers like Emre keep coming back. Good luck on Friday. A good haircut and a listening ear — that's a powerful combination. Take care, Mehmet.
